thinks.think: A tall white vertical sign featuring large black Japanese calligraphy stands against a cloudy sky. The scene transitions via a cross-dissolve, overlaying an interior view onto the sign. Red samurai armor figures become visible through the semi-transparent sign, creating a double-exposure effect where the exterior structure and interior display merge.

The scene shifts indoors to red samurai armor featuring detailed lacing and lacquered plates. A sword handle protrudes from the waist. In the background, a second red armor figure and a dark helmet with horns are visible under a patterned ceiling. Framed certificates line the wall. White text "The AJJ Movie File No.003" appears at the bottom left.

The camera tilts upward along the red samurai armor, revealing the upper chest, shoulders, and a detailed helmet with a crest. A bright light reflection glares off the left shoulder plate. Additional white text reading "Samurai Armor" and smaller Japanese characters appear at the bottom center, below the existing file number text.

The scene dissolves from the red armor display to a workshop interior. A person in blue clothing works on a wooden block, using a hand tool to manipulate the surface. New text overlays appear below the existing titles, reading "Armor Workshop" with Japanese characters, and "Marutake Sangyou" emerges at the bottom edge.

The view switches to a row of diverse samurai armor suits in black, red, and yellow displayed indoors. It then cuts to a close-up of hands weaving bright orange cords through reddish-brown lacquered plates. Large white text reading "Marutake Sangyou" overlays the bottom with Japanese characters beneath it.

The video cross-dissolves from a close-up of weaving orange cords to a display of a dark samurai armor suit. This figure features prominent, large golden horns on its helmet. Text overlays including "Marutake Sangyou" and "Armor Workshop" remain fixed at the bottom as the new armor becomes the primary focus.

Hands use red-handled pliers to manipulate a mesh of small metal rings in a detailed close-up. The scene transitions to a glossy red samurai helmet and mask featuring a distinct mustache, with a dark helmet visible in the background. White text overlays identifying the workshop and movie file number remain fixed at the bottom.

The red helmet view dissolves to reveal dark fabric adorned with intricate gold embroidery or scales. A hand enters the frame to touch the textured surface. Persistent white text overlays identifying the workshop and movie file remain centered, while a red and white logo watermark is visible in the bottom right corner.

The scene transitions to a static black title card featuring white text. Centered titles read "Samurai Armor", "Armor Workshop", and "Marutake Sangyou" alongside Japanese characters. "The AJJ Movie File No.003" appears top-left, "vol.1" is centered at the bottom, and a logo with a website URL sits in the bottom-right corner.

The black title card cuts to a workshop scene where an elderly man with grey hair, wearing a striped shirt, kneels on a blue cushion. He shapes a curved, light-colored piece of material on a wooden block, surrounded by shelves and tools. A website watermark is visible in the bottom right corner.

An elderly man in a striped shirt kneels on a blue cushion, hammering a curved, light-colored piece of material on a wooden block. The workshop background features shelves filled with tools and materials, while a website watermark remains in the bottom right corner.

The elderly man in a striped shirt continues kneeling on a blue cushion, repeatedly hammering a curved, light-colored piece of material secured on a wooden block. The cluttered workshop background with shelves and tools remains visible, and the website watermark stays in the bottom right corner.

A close-up shot shows hands holding a stack of thin, light-colored sheets on a wooden surface. A metal blade is positioned near the edge of the stack, and a grey-and-white striped sleeve is visible on the right. The website watermark stays in the bottom right corner.

A close-up shot focuses on hands manipulating a stack of thin, light-colored sheets on a wooden block. A metal blade is held against the edge of the material, appearing to trim or cut it. A grey-and-white striped sleeve is visible on the right, and the website watermark persists in the bottom right corner.

A close-up shows a person in a striped shirt hammering a curved, silver metal plate resting on a wooden block. The metal piece features a row of small holes along its edge. A wooden mallet strikes the surface of the shiny metal, shaping it within the workshop setting.

The scene continues with the metalworking process. A wooden mallet strikes a curved, silver metal plate resting on a wooden block. The plate features small holes along its edges. The craftsman's hand holds the piece steady while the other wields the mallet. The striped shirt sleeve is visible on the right.

A wooden mallet strikes a curved silver metal plate resting on a wooden block. The plate has small holes along its edges. The craftsman holds the metal steady while hammering it to shape. The striped sleeve of the worker is visible on the right. The website watermark remains in the bottom corner.

The hammering stops, revealing a collection of curved, silver metal armor plates arranged on a wooden surface. These pieces feature rows of small holes along their edges. White text overlays appear in the upper right, displaying a question in Japanese and English asking to tell about the materials of the workshop's armor.

Curved silver metal armor plates rest on a wooden workbench, featuring rows of small holes along their edges. New white text appears at the bottom reading "About 90% of our materials are metal; steel plate and aluminium." The previous question overlay regarding materials remains visible in the upper right corner.

The video cuts to a man with short dark hair in a black t-shirt speaking. Behind him, shelves are stacked with colorful armor parts and lacing cords. Subtitles at the bottom state that ninety percent of materials are metal, while a question overlay remains in the upper right corner regarding workshop materials.

A man with short dark hair wearing a black v-neck t-shirt speaks while facing forward. Behind him, shelves are stacked with colorful armor parts and lacing cords in red, green, and gold. White subtitles at the bottom state that ninety percent of materials are metal, while a question overlay remains in the upper right corner.

The man in the black v-neck t-shirt continues speaking directly to the camera, his mouth moving and facial expression shifting slightly. Behind him, the shelves densely packed with colorful armor components and lacing cords remain static. The white subtitles regarding metal materials and the question overlay in the upper right corner persist on screen without change.

A man in a black v-neck t-shirt continues speaking in front of shelves stacked with colorful armor components. The white subtitles at the bottom change to read "Other than that we use compressed paper; a very dense, hardened paper." The question overlay regarding workshop materials remains visible in the upper right corner.

The man in the black v-neck t-shirt continues speaking, his mouth open and teeth visible. The white subtitles at the bottom describing compressed paper remain unchanged, along with the question overlay in the upper right. Behind him, the shelves stacked with colorful armor parts and lacing cords remain static.

The man in the black v-neck t-shirt continues speaking, his gaze shifting slightly downward. The white subtitles describing the use of dense, hardened paper remain at the bottom of the screen. The question overlay regarding workshop materials persists in the upper right corner against the background of colorful armor components.

The man in the black v-neck shirt continues speaking in front of shelves filled with colorful armor components. The white subtitles at the bottom change to display new text: "We take variety of requests from customers. Upgrading would cost additional, but depending on their order we can use traditional materials such as leather." The question overlay in the upper right corner remains static.

The scene cuts to a silver, dome-shaped metal helmet bowl resting on a cluttered workbench. Surrounding the helmet are tools including a black marker, black gloves, and small gold ornaments. The previous subtitles regarding customer requests and leather materials remain at the bottom, while the interview question overlay persists in the upper right corner.

summary.text: [0-2] A tall white vertical sign with large black Japanese calligraphy stands outside, dissolving to overlay an interior view of red samurai armor figures. [2-6] The scene shifts indoors to a red samurai armor suit with detailed lacing and a sword handle; the camera tilts up to the helmet as text 'The AJJ Movie File No.003' and 'Samurai Armor' appears. [6-8] The view dissolves to a workshop where a person in blue works on wood, adding text 'Armor Workshop' and 'Marutake Sangyou'. [8-12] A row of diverse armors is shown, followed by a close-up of hands weaving orange cords into plates. The video concludes with a dark armor featuring large golden horns, overlaid with 'Marutake Sangyou' text.

[12-16] Hands use red-handled pliers on metal ring mesh, transitioning to a glossy red samurai helmet with a mustache; a dark helmet sits in the background. Text overlays read "The AJJ Movie File No.003", "Samurai Armor", "Armor Workshop", "Marutake Sangyou", and Japanese characters "鎧", "甲冑工房", "丸武産業". The scene dissolves to dark fabric with gold embroidery touched by a hand, with an "A-JANAIKA JAPAN" logo and URL appearing.
[16-18] A black title card displays "The AJJ Movie File No.003", "Samurai Armor", "Armor Workshop", "Marutake Sangyou", "vol.1", and corresponding Japanese text, alongside the logo and URL.
[18-26] An elderly man with grey hair in a striped shirt kneels on a blue cushion in a workshop, hammering a curved light-colored piece on a wooden block. Shelves with tools fill the background. A close-up shows hands holding thin sheets near a metal blade. The URL watermark persists.

[26-28] Close-up of hands manipulating thin, light-colored sheets on a wooden block while a metal blade trims the edge; a grey-and-white striped sleeve is visible near the watermark "A-JANAIKA JAPAN www.a-janaika-japan.com". [28-34] A person in a striped shirt uses a wooden mallet to repeatedly hammer a curved, silver metal plate featuring small holes along its edge; the craftsman's hand holds the piece steady on a wooden block to shape the shiny metal. [34-36] The hammering stops, revealing a collection of curved, silver metal armor plates with rivet holes arranged on a wooden surface with text overlay: "御社製作の鎧・甲冑の素材、材質についてお聞かせください - Tell us about the materials of your workshop's armor." [36-38] Additional white text appears at the bottom: "About 90% of our materials are metal; steel plate and aluminium." [38-40] A man with short dark hair in a black t-shirt speaks directly to the camera in front of shelves densely stacked with colorful armor parts and intricate lacing cords, with the previous text overlays remaining visible.

[40-44] A man with short dark hair wearing a black v-neck t-shirt speaks facing forward. Behind him, shelves are stacked with colorful armor parts and lacing cords in red, green, and gold. White subtitles state: "About 90% of our materials are metal; steel plate and aluminium." A question overlay remains in the upper right.
[44-50] The man continues speaking, his gaze shifting slightly downward. The white subtitles change to: "Other than that we use compressed paper; a very dense, hardened paper." The question overlay regarding workshop materials persists against the background of colorful armor components.
[50-52] The man speaks as subtitles update to: "We take variety of requests from customers. Upgrading would cost additional, but depending on their order we can use traditional materials such as leather." The question overlay in the upper right corner remains static while the colorful armor background is visible.

[52-56] A silver, dome-shaped metal helmet bowl rests on a cluttered workbench surrounded by a black marker, black gloves, and small gold ornaments. [56-60] The view cuts to a man in a black t-shirt and grey gloves working on a small item at the bench; the silver bowl and a board displaying armor photos sit to his left against shelves of tools. [60-64] The scene shifts to a side view of a person in dark clothing and khaki pants operating a power tool near a green vise and corrugated wall, followed by a cross-dissolve transition showing semi-transparent hands. Throughout, subtitles read: "We take variety of requests from customers. Upgrading would cost additional, but depending on their order we can use traditional materials such as leather," with the overlay "Tell us about the materials of your workshop's armor."

[64-68] Close-up of hands holding a silver tool against a curved metal piece with a green vise in the background; white text overlays ask about materials and describe customer requests. [68-76] Cut to a man in a black t-shirt speaking in front of shelves stacked with colorful armor parts. The upper right text changes to ask, "How many models of samurai armor do your workshop has right now?" Subtitles appear at the bottom reading, "Well, currently we have hundreds of variations; small chigo 'baby armor', big Busho armor and our original armor."

[76-84] A man in a black t-shirt speaks before a cross-dissolve reveals a workshop with a workbench, metal pieces, and a green vise where a person in khaki pants stands. The view shifts to a person with short brown hair and glasses in a beige vest working on chainmail on a wooden frame, followed by close-ups of hands using red-handled pliers on the links. Static text overlays read: "Well, currently we have hundreds of variations; small chigo '稚児' baby armor, big Busho armor and our original armor." and "How many models of samurai armor do your workshop has right now?"
[84-88] As silver chainmail hangs from the wooden structure, the bottom subtitle updates to: "We can make about 150 different models right now."
[88-90] The scene transitions to a glossy black helmet adorned with gold floral patterns and yellow braided cords. The overlay text changes to: "いまおっしゃっていた『領』とは鎧や甲冑の数え方でしょうか？ - How do you count armor in Japanese?" while the bottom subtitle reads: "We use Ryo '領'. one Ryo, two Ryo, three Ryo..."

[90-94] Two black, antler-shaped ornaments stand on a white surface before grey shelves filled with vertical wooden planks featuring Japanese calligraphy. Overlays ask "- How do you count armor in Japanese?" and explain "'領' as in first kanji of Ryodo '領土': one's property." [94-98] The view zooms in on the wooden planks and a blue board with characters, retaining the text overlays. [98-102] The scene changes to a weathered green wooden board with a rectangular indentation next to a purple board with a white geometric crest. The bottom subtitles disappear, leaving only the logo and website URL.

[104-112] An older man with glasses, wearing a black t-shirt and dark apron, works at a cluttered bench on a glossy red helmet. He lifts and lowers a large bundle of green lacing cords. Text overlays ask, "どのような鎧や甲冑の製作にも対応するのでしょうか？ - What kind of custom order can you take for the Samurai armor?" and explain, "We first carefully listen to our customer's design requests and budget, then we work accordingly to satisfy their needs."
[112-116] A man in glasses and a black "YOKOIYAMA" shirt wears white gloves while manipulating a black curved armor piece; a silver helmet bowl sits behind him. Text asks, "これまでに受けたオファーでとくに印象に残っているものは？ - What is the most memorable armor that you've worked on?"
[116-118] A woman with long brown hair in a light green sleeveless top and brown apron assembles yellow armor plates with blue lacing. The subtitle reads, "It was from the museum."

[120-126] Close-ups show hands threading blue cords through yellow perforated armor plates on a grey table. The worker wears a light green top and brown apron, with red-handled scissors nearby. Top text reads: "これまでに受けたオファーでとくに印象に残っているものは？ - What is the most memorable armor that you've worked on?" Bottom subtitles state: "It was of reproduction project of an actual armor worn by historical samurai generals." [126-130] A person in a white shirt and black apron works on dark armor with brown patterned fabric, manipulating black lacing. A close-up shows a hand with a gold ring pressing glossy cords. Bottom subtitles update: "It required our best effort to faithfully reproduce its high quality historical armors."

[130-136] Extreme close-ups feature a hand wearing a silver ring pressing black, glossy lacing cords adorned with white symbols against brown fabric with gold swirls. The hands thread and press the intricate cords onto the armor structure. Text overlays remain static: "What is the most memorable armor that you've worked on?" (top right) and "It required our best effort to faithfully reproduce its high quality historical armors" (bottom). [136-138] The view shifts inside a black helmet structure where hands manipulate a grey fabric lining with small dots. Blue and pink plastic clothespins clamp the lining to the black rim, securing it as the worker adjusts the interior fit.

[140-142] Hands adjust grey dotted fabric lining inside a black helmet bowl held by blue clothespins. A subtitle appears: "Well, most of them are original, as we adjust our armor to fit their body perfectly," answering the top text: "What kind of order comes from foreign customers?" [142-146] The view changes to a dark, curved armor piece with horizontal laced scales on a blue surface next to a white cloth. [146-148] The camera pans right, revealing two long, black curved plates with holes on the white cloth, while the laced armor remains partially visible on the left. The subtitles regarding foreign orders and body adjustments remain fixed on screen.

[118-120] A woman in a light green top and brown apron assembles yellow armor plates with blue lacing, using a tool on cords, while subtitles note the project reproduces armor of historical samurai generals. [138-140] Hands adjust grey fabric lining with black dots inside a black helmet bowl, secured by blue and pink clothespins, as text asks, "外国の方々からのオーダーはどのようなものが多いですか？ - What kind of order comes from foreign customers?" [148-156] A hand touches and lifts long, curved black metal plates with perforated holes on a white cloth near dark laced armor. Subtitles explain orders are often original to fit bodies perfectly, or for martial art practice like Enbu.

[156-168] A hand lifts and manipulates a long, curved black metal plate with perforated holes on a white cloth surface; later, a gloved hand wipes and adjusts the plate before placing it down. A second similar black plate lies stationary to the left near dark laced armor components resting on a blue mat. Fixed subtitles appear throughout: Top right asks "外国の方々からのオーダーはどのようなものが多いですか？ - What kind of order comes from foreign customers?". Bottom left states "The majority of them get their armor to wear around, in martial art practice such as Enbu “演武”.". Bottom right displays "A-JANAIKA JAPAN! www.a-janaika-japan.com".

[170-178] A gloved hand wipes a long, curved black metal plate resting on a white cloth, briefly lifting its right end. Another perforated black plate lies stationary to the left near dark fabric bundles. Subtitles explaining orders for martial art practice like Enbu remain fixed at the bottom. [178-180] The scene cuts to a workspace where a hand cleans a vertical gold strip with a white cloth. A green cutting mat below holds yellow rectangular pieces, a paintbrush, and scissors. Subtitles regarding foreign orders and martial arts practice remain overlaid on the screen.

[180-184] A man in a dark t-shirt kneels on a tatami mat with red samurai armor pieces arranged on the floor before him, while rows of various armors stand in the background. Text overlays ask "How one should put armors on display?" [184-188] The man continues to kneel facing forward with the red armor pieces, including a helmet and chest plate, visible on the mat. A subtitle appears at the bottom reading "The ideal way is to make it look as if it's worn by human."

[190-198] A kneeling man in a dark t-shirt picks up a red arm armor piece from the floor, lifting the cylindrical object with both hands to examine it. Subtitles appear stating, "For example, put some paper stuffing in these arm and leg armor parts to make it lifelike." He holds the red armor piece to display its shape and interior, then moves it toward a red chest armor standing on the mat, positioning it near the shoulder area. He lifts the red lacquered component upwards to show it again before lowering it back onto the tatami, all while the instructional text remains on screen.

[198-204] The man places a red arm armor piece onto the tatami mat and returns to a kneeling position with hands on his thighs. The red chest armor stands upright in the center amidst other components. The subtitle "For example, put some paper stuffing in these arm and leg armor parts to make it lifelike." is displayed at the bottom. [204-206] The instructional subtitle disappears. The scene cuts to a close-up of a black samurai armor suit, focusing on the leg guards and waist area which features a gold circular crest. The camera pans slightly upwards along the armor's torso.

[206-210] The camera tilts upwards along a black samurai armor display, moving from the waist area with a gold crest up to the chest and neck guard. Decorative cords with large gold beads and red tassels hang from the shoulders. The tilt reveals the full helmet featuring large curved horns and a white cord across the face mask. The view expands to show the ceiling above, which has patterned panels and hanging lights, framing the top of the armor display. [210-214] The scene cuts to a wide shot revealing a row of colorful samurai armors lined up indoors. Several helmets feature large, golden Japanese kanji characters mounted on top. Behind the display, traditional shoji screen walls are visible, replacing the previous close-up. The video maintains this static wide shot, showing the nearest armor has blue and green lacing. The watermark "A-JANAIKA JAPAN" and URL "www.a-janaika-japan.com" are visible in the bottom right corner.

[28-34] A person in a striped shirt uses a wooden mallet to repeatedly hammer a curved, silver metal plate featuring small holes along its edge; the craftsman's hand holds the piece steady on a wooden block to shape the shiny metal. [6-8] The view dissolves to a workshop where a person in blue works on wood, adding text 'Armor Workshop' and 'Marutake Sangyou'. [8-12] A row of diverse armors is shown, followed by a close-up of hands weaving orange cords into plates. The video concludes with a dark armor featuring large golden horns, overlaid with 'Marutake Sangyou' text. [12-16] Hands use red-handled pliers on metal ring mesh, transitioning to a glossy red samurai helmet with a mustache; a dark helmet sits in the background. Text overlays read "The AJJ Movie File No.003", "Samurai Armor", "Armor Workshop", "Marutake Sangyou", and Japanese characters "鎧", "甲冑工房", "丸武産業". The scene dissolves to dark fabric with gold embroidery touched by a hand, with an "A-JANAIKA JAPAN" logo and URL appearing. [16-18] A black title card displays "The AJJ Movie File No.003", "Samurai Armor", "Armor Workshop", "Marutake

[64-68] Close-up of hands holding a silver tool against a curved metal piece with a green vise in the background; white text overlays ask about materials and describe customer requests. [68-76] Cut to a man in a black t-shirt speaking in front of shelves stacked with colorful armor parts. The upper right text changes to ask, "How many models of samurai armor do your workshop has right now?" Subtitles appear at the bottom reading, "Well, currently we have hundreds of variations; small chigo 'baby armor', big Busho armor and our original armor."

[76-84] A man in a black t-shirt speaks before a cross-dissolve reveals a workshop with a workbench, metal pieces, and a green vise where a person in khaki pants stands. The view shifts to a person with short brown hair and glasses in a beige vest working on chainmail on a wooden frame, followed by close-ups of hands using red-handled pliers on the links. Static text overlays read: "Well, currently we have hundreds of variations; small chigo '稚児' baby armor, big Busho armor and our original armor." and "How many models of samurai armor do your workshop has right now?"
[84-88] As silver chainmail hangs from the wooden structure, the bottom subtitle updates to: "We can make about 150 different models right now."
[88-90] The scene transitions to a glossy black helmet adorned with gold floral patterns and yellow braided cords. The overlay text changes to: "いまおっしゃっていた『領』とは鎧や甲冑の数え方でしょうか？ - How do you count armor in Japanese?" while the bottom subtitle reads: "We use Ryo '領'. one Ryo, two Ryo, three Ryo..."

[90-94] Two black, antler-shaped ornaments stand on a white surface before grey shelves filled with vertical wooden planks featuring Japanese calligraphy. Overlays ask "- How do you count armor in Japanese?" and explain "'領' as in first kanji of Ryodo '領土': one's property." [94-98] The view zooms in on the wooden planks and a blue board with characters, retaining the text overlays. [98-102] The scene changes to a weathered green wooden board with a rectangular indentation next to a purple board with a white geometric crest. The bottom subtitles disappear, leaving only the logo and website URL.

[104-112] An older man with glasses, wearing a black t-shirt and dark apron, works at a cluttered bench on a glossy red helmet. He lifts and lowers a large bundle of green lacing cords. Text overlays ask, "どのような鎧や甲冑の製作にも対応するのでしょうか？ - What kind of custom order can you take for the Samurai armor?" and explain, "We first carefully listen to our customer's design requests and budget, then we work accordingly to satisfy their needs."
[112-116] A man in glasses and a black "YOKOIYAMA" shirt wears white gloves while manipulating a black curved armor piece; a silver helmet bowl sits behind him. Text asks, "これまでに受けたオファーでとくに印象に残っているものは？ - What is the most memorable armor that you've worked on?"
[116-118] A woman with long brown hair in a light green sleeveless top and brown apron assembles yellow armor plates with blue lacing. The subtitle reads, "It was from the museum."

recent_thinks.text: The scene cuts to a workshop interior where a man with dark hair, wearing a blue and white shirt and dark apron, sits at a workbench. He manipulates small items surrounded by gold-colored metal armor plates, wires, and tools. A fan and drill press stand behind him near shelves.

A woman wearing a light green top and brown apron assembles yellow armor plates with blue lacing. The view shifts to a close-up of her hands using a tool on the cords. Subtitles update to state the project was a reproduction of armor worn by historical samurai generals.

[6-8] The view dissolves to a workshop where a person in blue works on wood, adding text 'Armor Workshop' and 'Marutake Sangyou'. [8-12] A row of diverse armors is shown, followed by a close-up of hands weaving orange cords into plates. The video concludes with a dark armor featuring large golden horns, overlaid with 'Marutake Sangyou' text. [12-16] Hands use red-handled pliers on metal ring mesh, transitioning to a glossy red samurai helmet with a mustache; a dark helmet sits in the background. Text overlays read "The AJJ Movie File No.003", "Samurai Armor", "Armor Workshop", "Marutake Sangyou", and Japanese characters "鎧", "甲冑工房", "丸武産業". The scene dissolves to dark fabric with gold embroidery touched by a hand, with an "A-JANAIKA JAPAN" logo and URL appearing. [16-18] A black title card displays "The AJJ Movie File No.003", "Samurai Armor", "Armor Workshop", "Marutake

[120-126] Close-ups show hands threading blue cords through yellow perforated armor plates on a grey table. The worker wears a light green top and brown apron, with red-handled scissors nearby. Top text reads: "これまでに受けたオファーでとくに印象に残っているものは？ - What is the most memorable armor that you've worked on?" Bottom subtitles state: "It was of reproduction project of an actual armor worn by historical samurai generals." [126-130] A person in a white shirt and black apron works on dark armor with brown patterned fabric, manipulating black lacing. A close-up shows a hand with a gold ring pressing glossy cords. Bottom subtitles update: "It required our best effort to faithfully reproduce its high quality historical armors."

[130-136] Extreme close-ups feature a hand wearing a silver ring pressing black, glossy lacing cords adorned with white symbols against brown fabric with gold swirls. The hands thread and press the intricate cords onto the armor structure. Text overlays remain static: "What is the most memorable armor that you've worked on?" (top right) and "It required our best effort to faithfully reproduce its high quality historical armors" (bottom). [136-138] The view shifts inside a black helmet structure where hands manipulate a grey fabric lining with small dots. Blue and pink plastic clothespins clamp the lining to the black rim, securing it as the worker adjusts the interior fit.

[140-142] Hands adjust grey dotted fabric lining inside a black helmet bowl held by blue clothespins. A subtitle appears: "Well, most of them are original, as we adjust our armor to fit their body perfectly," answering the top text: "What kind of order comes from foreign customers?" [142-146] The view changes to a dark, curved armor piece with horizontal laced scales on a blue surface next to a white cloth. [146-148] The camera pans right, revealing two long, black curved plates with holes on the white cloth, while the laced armor remains partially visible on the left. The subtitles regarding foreign orders and body adjustments remain fixed on screen.

[118-120] A woman in a light green top and brown apron assembles yellow armor plates with blue lacing, using a tool on cords, while subtitles note the project reproduces armor of historical samurai generals. [138-140] Hands adjust grey fabric lining with black dots inside a black helmet bowl, secured by blue and pink clothespins, as text asks, "外国の方々からのオーダーはどのようなものが多いですか？ - What kind of order comes from foreign customers?" [148-156] A hand touches and lifts long, curved black metal plates with perforated holes on a white cloth near dark laced armor. Subtitles explain orders are often original to fit bodies perfectly, or for martial art practice like Enbu.

recent_thinks.text: The scene cuts to a workshop interior where a man with dark hair, wearing a blue and white shirt and dark apron, sits at a workbench. He manipulates small items surrounded by gold-colored metal armor plates, wires, and tools. A fan and drill press stand behind him near shelves.

A hand lifts a long, curved black metal plate with perforated holes from a white cloth surface. Another similar plate lies nearby. To the left, dark laced armor components rest on a blue mat. Subtitles regarding foreign customer orders and body adjustments remain fixed at the bottom.

A hand lifts a long, curved black metal plate with perforated holes over a white cloth surface. Another similar plate rests nearby. To the left, dark laced armor components sit on a blue mat. Subtitles regarding foreign customer orders and body adjustments remain fixed at the bottom of the screen.

A gloved hand manipulates a long, curved black metal plate with perforated holes over a white cloth surface. Another similar black plate lies stationary to the left near dark laced armor components. Subtitles at the bottom explain that customers often order armor for martial art practice such as Enbu.

A gloved hand wipes or adjusts a long, curved black metal plate with perforated holes on a white cloth. Another similar black plate rests to the left near dark laced armor components. Subtitles at the bottom explain that customers often order armor for martial art practice such as Enbu.
